Fern PolrrrcAl PnecrrcEs CoMMrssroN
-ll8 J Street o Suire 620 o gncynnrento. CA 95gll_2329
(916) 321-5660 o Fax (916) -322-0886
September 16,2009
Dean Glaser
Councilmember
City of Fortuna
1546 Ronald Ave.
Fortuna, CA 95540
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-09-210
Dear Mr. Glaser:
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").r Nothing in this letter should be
construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any
conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions. tn addition, this
letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (,.the
Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (ln re Oglesby
(r975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)
Please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We
therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws
such as common law conflict of interest or Govemment Code Section 1090.
QrrEsTroN
May you make, participate in making, or influence a govemmental decision
regarding a proposed building for a business that will offer marine outboard engine repair
despite owning a business specializing in marine outboard engine repair approx-imately
five miles away from rhe proposed building?
' The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections g1000 through 91014. All
statutory ref'erences are to the Government Code. unless otherwise indicated. The regulationi of the Fair
Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections l8 I l0 through I U997 of Title 2 of the Califbrnia
Code of Regulations. All regulatory ret'erences are to Title 2. Division 6 of the California Code of
Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.
;i-


File No. A-09-2lO
Page No. 2
CONCLUSION
You may participate in the decision regarding the proposed building so long as no
additional facts establish a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your
economic interest(s).
FACTS
You are a member of the Fortuna City Council. You are also the owner of a small
business known as Fortuna Marine. This business specializes in the repair of outboard
marine engines and is limited to engines manufactured by Johnson/Evinrude between
1953 and the 1990's.
As a member of the Fortuna City Council you may be considering a building
proposed by Ken Molton, owner of a business known u, iFull-On Auto.i Full-on Auto,
which plans to occupy the building at the end of 8th street if approved, specializes in
repairing all internal combustion engines including both automotive and marine engines.
It also offers various mechanical services including custom fabrication and weldinf.
In a telephone conversation on September 2,2009, you further stated that Full-On
Auto intends to relocate to the proposed building from its current location in Eureka,
California, which is approximately twenty miles from the City of Fortuna. your
business, Fortuna Marine, is located approximately five miles from the proposed building
and is currently the only outboard marine engine repair shop in the Cityof Fortuna. In
addition, you have stated that Fortuna Marine is a small business op"rat"d from your
residence and will typically handle no more than one repair job peimonth. you estimate
Fortuna Marine's annual gross sales at approximately $3,000 to $4,000 and annual
expenses at $6,000 to $7,000. Under the facts you have provided, Fortuna Marine is
operated for a loss of approximately $2,000 to $4,000 per year.
AIIALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the governmental decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the
public official's economic inrerests. (section 87103; Regulation 1g700(a).) The
Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an
individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision, which
we apply to your question. (Regularion 18700(bXl)-(g).)


File No. 4-09-210
Page No. 3
Step One: Are you a "public official?r'
The Act's conflict-of- interest provisions apply only to "public officials."
(Sections 87100, 87103; Regulation 18700(bXl).) A "public ofiicial" is "every member,
officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency . . ..,'
(Section 82048.) As a member of the Fortuna City Council, you are a public official
within the meaning of the Act.2
Step Two: Are you making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental
decision?
A public official "makes a govemmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.1) A public official
"participates in a governmental decision" when, acting within the auihority of his or her
position and without significant intervening substantive review, the official negotiates,
advises, or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the govenimental
decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use hii or her official
position to influence a decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the official contacts or
appears before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of,his or her agency.
(Regulation 18702.3.) You are making, participating in making, or influen"irg u
governmental decision when acting in your role as a member of the Fortuna City Council.
Step Three: What are your.,economic interests?r,
Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a
govemmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official, a member of his or her immediate family,3 or on any of the offi"iul', economic
interests, described as follows:
a
An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or
indirect investment of $2,000 or more (Section g7103(a); Regulation lg703.l(a));
2 If a public official's office is listed in Section 87200 (*8ZzDfilers" include members of a city
council) and he or she has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she
must: ( l) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, verbally identify each type oieconomic interest
involved in the decision as well as details of the economic intereit, as disiussed in Regulation
18702.5(bX I )(B), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) l-eave the room for rhe
duration ofthe discussion and/or vote on the iiem. For closed sessions, consent calendars, absences and
speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests, special rules found in Regulation lg:oz.5,
subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Section 87105.)
I "Immediate family" is limited to the official's "spouse and dependent children', under
Section 82029.


a
a
File No. A-09-210
Page No. 4
or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any
position of management (Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.1(b)).
An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b); Regulation 19703.2.)
a
a
mterests
Real Property
You have stated that Fortuna Marine is operated from your personal residence
Assuming your inteiest in your personal residence is $2,000 or more, you have an
economic interest in this real properry. (Section 87103(b).)
Fortuna Marine
As the owner of Fortuna Marine, it appears that you have an investment of $2,000
or more in the business and are also a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or
manager of this business. Therefore, you have an economic interest in nortuna Marine as
a business entity. (Section 87103 (a) and (d).) In addition, you will have received
income of $500 or more from Fortuna Marine in the 12 months prior to the governmental
decision. Therefore, you also have an economic interest in Fortuna Marine as a source of
income. (Section 87102(c).)
Clients or Customers of Fortuna Marine
As the owner of Fortuna Marine, you may also have economic interests in the
clients or customers of this business as sources of income. Under Section 82030(a), if a
public official owns a lO-percent interest or greater in a business, customers who are
sources of income to that business are also considered sources of income to the public
official. Provided that your pro rata share of the income derived from any particular
An economic interest in a source of income, including promised income, which
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section
87103(c); Regulation I 8703.3.)
An economic interest in a source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to
$420 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 97103(et
Regulation 18703.4.)
An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her
immediate family. This is known as the "personal financial effects', rule.
(Section 87 103; Regulation I 8703.5.)
From your account of the facts, you appear to have the following economic


File No. 4-09-210
Page No. 5
client or customer aggregates to $500 or more in the 12 months prior to the governmental
decision, you also have an economic interest in the client or customer as a source of
income. (Section 87103(c).)a
Personal Finances
A public official always has an economic interest in his or her personal finances.
A governmental decision will have an effect on this economic interestlf the decision will
result in the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of the official or his or her
immediate family increasing or decreasing. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.)5
Step Four: Are your economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the
decision?
Real Property
Regulation 18704.2(a) states that real property in which a public official has an
economic interest is directly involved in a governmental decision if any of the following
apply:
"(1) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any part
of that real property, is located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or the
proposed boundaries) of the property which is the subject of the
governmental decision . . ..
"(2) The govemmental decision involves the zoning or rezoning , 
'
annexation or deannexation, sale, purchase, or lease, or inclusion in or
exclusion from any city, county, district or other local governmental
subdivision, of the real property in which the official has an interest or a
similar decision affecting the real property. . ..
u However, you have not identified any particular client or customer of Fortuna Marine.
Therefbre, we cannot analyze any potential .onfiittr of interest you may have in a decision affecting a
client or customer. Prior to making, participating in making, or influeniing any governmental decislon you
will need to determine whether any of the clients or customers of Fortuna tvtarine qualify as a source of
income and whether a conflict of interest may arise based upon an economic intereit in one of these clients
or customers. If you have additional questions related to any economic interests in a client or customer of
Fortuna Marine, it is advisable that you request turther assistance providing all relevant facts.
5 A financial efTect on the value of real property owned directly or indirectly by a public official,
and a financial effect onrhe gross revenues, expenses, or value ofassets and liabilities ofa business entity
in which a public ofticial has a direct or indirect investment interest, are not considered separate financial
e-tfects on the official's personal finances and would not be analyzed separately under the:.personal
financial etfects" rule. (Regulation 18705.5(a).) Accordingly, the personal financial efTecti rule does not
appear to apply to your circumstances and we will not discuss it further.


File No. 4-09-210
Page No.6
"(3) The governmental decision involves the issuance, denial or
revocation of a license, permit or other land use entitlement authorizing a
specific use or uses of the real property in which the official has an intJrest.
"(4) The governmental decision involves the imposition, repeal, or
modification of any taxes or fees assessed or imposed on the real pioperty in
which the official has an interest.
"(5) The governmental decision is to designate the survey area, [o
select the project area, to adopt the preliminary plan, to form a project area
committee, to certify the environmental document, to adopt the
redevelopment plan, to add territory to the redevelopm"nia.ea, or to rescind
or amend any of the above decisions; and real property in which the official
has an interest, or any part of it is located within the bLundaries (or the
proposed boundaries) of the redevelopment area.
"(6) The decision involves construction of, or improvements to,
streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities, and the real
property in which the official has an interest will receive new or improved
services."
Because your residence, from which you operate Fortuna Marine, is not located
within 500 feet of the property that is the subject of the governmental decision and you
have not provided any facts that the govemmental decision involves any of the typei of
decisions outlined in Regulation 18704.2(aX2)-(6), it appears that yourLconomic interest
in your residence is tndirectly involved in the gou"*rnlrtal decision.6
Fortuna Marine
Regulation 18704.1(a) states that a business entity or source of income is directly
involved in a decision before the official's agency when ihat business entity or source of
income, either directly or by an agent:
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by
filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
"(2) [s a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
conceming the decision before the official or the officiai's ug.n.yl A
person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance,
renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other
entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person.',
u "Ifapublicofficial'seconomicinterestisnotdirectlyinvolvedinagovernmental 
decision,itis
indirectly involved." (Regulation 18704(a).)


File No. 4-09-210
Page No. 7
Business entities and sources of income that are not directly involved in
governmental decisions are regarded as indirectly involved. (Regulations 18704.1(b),
18705.1(aX2), and Regulation 18705.3(b).) From the facts provided, there is no
indication that Fortuna Marine initiated the proceeding or is a party in, or subject of, the
proceeding within the meaning of Regulation 18704.1. Accordingly, it appears as though
your economic interests in Fortuna Marine, as both a business entity and a source of
income, are indirec,r/y involved in the govemment decision.
Steps Five and Six: Will there be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on your economic interests?
Materiality
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of a
govemmental decision on a public official's economic interests is material. (Regulation
18700(a).) Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on an economic interest will be material, depending on ih" nature of the
economic interest and whether that interest is directly or indirectly involved in the
agency's decision.
Real Property
For properties indirectly involved in a governmental decision, Regulation
18705.2(b)(l) provides that the financial effect is presumed not to be maGrial. However,
this presumption may be rebutted by proof that there are specific circumstances regarding
the governmental decision, its financial effect, and the nature of the real property in
which the public official has an economic interest that make it reasonabty foreseeable the
decision will have a material financial effect on the real property in whic-h the official has
an interest. Examples of these specific circumstances include, but are not limited to,
circumstances where the decision affects:
"(A) The development potential or income producing potential of
the real property in which the official has an economic interest;
"(B) The use of the real property in which the official has an
economic interest;
"(c) The character of the neighborhood including, but not limited
to, substantial effects on: traffic, view, privacy, intensity of use, noise
levels, air emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood.',


File No. 4-09-210
Page No. 8
Fortuna Marine
For economic interests in business entities indirectly involved in a decision,
including business entities, which are a source of income to an official, the materiality
standard is given at Regulation 18705.1(c).
The thresholds for materiality under this regulation vary with the size of the
business. Based upon the facts you have provided, Regulation 18705.1(c)(4) provides the
applicable materiality threshold for a relatively small business such as Fortuna Marine.
Under this regulation, the financial effect of a govemmental decision on a business is
material if it is reasonably foreseeable that:
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the
amount of $20,000 or more; or,
. 
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating
existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or,
"(C) The govemmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of
$20,000 or more."
Foreseeability
Once a public official has determined the materiality standard applicable to each
of his or her economic interests, the next step is determining whether it ii "reasonably
foreseeable" that the standard will be met. A material finarrcial effect on an economic
interest is "reasonably foreseeable" if it is substantially likely that one or more of the
materiality standards will be met as a result of the govemmental decision. (Regulation
18706(a).) An effect need not be certain to be considered "reasonably foreseeable," but it
must be more than a mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1g75) I Fppc ops. l9g.)
Ultimately, whether a material financial effect is foreseeable at the time a decision
is made depends on facts and circumstances peculiar to each case. (/n re Thorner, supra,
at 198') Because the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice (/n
re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 7l), the foreseeability of a particular financial effect is a
determination that must be left, in most instances, to the informed judgment of the public
official.


File No. 4-09-210
Page No.9
Real Propertv
Your property is approximately five miles from Full-on Auto's proposed
building. Barring additional information indicating special circumstan"Lr, ih" financial
effect of the governmental decision on your economic interest in real property is
presumed not to be material under Regulation 18705.2(b)(l). Moreorer, *" irur"
previously advised that the presumption that an indirectly involved property will not be
materially affected is rebutted only if the governmental decision will hive a clearly
distinguishable and substantial financial effect on an official's property. (yang Advice
Letter, No. I-06-198 and Berger Advice Letter, No. A-05-054.) In adopting Regulation
18705.2(b)(l), the Commission noted that "beyond the 500 foot range, participation
would be allowed unless something about the decision makes the official's property
'stick out like a sore thumb."' (Commission Minutes of Meeting, October 6,2iJ00, ut
page 4.)
The only fact you have provided that suggests a special circumstance potentially
rebutting the presumption of non-materiality, is the fact that Fortuna Marine is currently
the only outboard marine engine repair shop in the City of Fortuna. However, this
special circumstance is mitigated by the following facts: (1) Full-On Auto provides a full
range of mechanical and fabrication services, (2) Full-On Auto is currentlyoperating in
the city of Eureka just twenty miles from the City of Fortuna, (3) Fortuna tutuii6 onty
repairs engines manufactured by Johnson/Evinrude between 1953 and the 1990,s, ura 1+;
Fortuna Marine is a small business operated from your residence typically handling jusi
one repair job per month. From the facts provided, there is no indicition of special
circumstances to rebut the presumption of non-materiality. Accordingly, it does not
appear reasonably foreseeable that the decision regarding Full-On Auto's proposed
building will have a material financial effect on your economic interest inieai properry.
Fortuna Marine:
As provided in Regulation 18705.1(c)(4), a financial effect of a govemmental
decision of less than $20,000 on the gross revenues of a business entity,-g5,000 on the
expenses of a business entity, or $20,000 on a business entity,s assets or liabilities is not
considered material. Under the facts you have provided, Fortuna Marine,s gross
revenues are just $3,000 to $4,000 a year, while its expenses are $6,000 to $?,000 ayear
Considering your specific circumstances and Fortuna Marine's minimal size, it does not
appear reasonably foreseeable that the govemmental decision regarding Full-On Auto's
proposed building would affect Fortuna Marine's gross revenues, assets, or liabilities at
the $20,000 materiality threshold.
In addition,:r:l though Fortuna Marine's annual expenses exceed $5,000, they
only do so by $1,000-$3,000 per year. Thus, considering your specific circumstances, it
seems equally unlikely that the decision regarding Full-ON Auto's proposed building
would add to or affect these expenses in an amount meeting or e*ceiding the $5,000


File No. 4.-09-210
Page No. 10
materiality threshold. Accordingly, we do not think it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect on your economic intirest in Fortuna
Marine.
Steps Seven and Eight: Does this governmental decision come within any exception
to the conflict-of-interest rules?
Even if an official has a conflict of interest, disqualification is not required if the
govemmental decision affects the public official's economic interests in a manner that is
indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision'will affect the public generally.
(Section 87103; Regulation 18707(a).)
Additionally, in certain rare circumstances, a public official may be called upon to
take part in a decision despite the fact that the official may have a disqualifying conflict of
interest under the "legally required participation" exception. This exception uf,pti* only in
certain very specific circumstances where the government agency would Ue paratyzed fiom
acting. (Section 87101; Regulation 18708.)
However, you have not presented any facts indicating that the "public generally,,
or the "legally required participation" exceptions are applicable to youicirc,,rirtun""r, ,o
we will not address them further.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
By: Brian G. Lau
Counsel, Legal Division
BGLjgl

You may participate in the decision regarding the proposed building so long as no
additional facts establish a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your
economic interest(s).

sections.analysis:
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the governmental decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the
public official's economic inrerests. (section 87103; Regulation 1g700(a).) The
Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an
individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision, which
we apply to your question. (Regularion 18700(bXl)-(g).)
Step One: Are you a "public official?r'
The Act's conflict-of- interest provisions apply only to "public officials."
(Sections 87100, 87103; Regulation 18700(bXl).) A "public ofiicial" is "every member,
officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency . . ..,'
(Section 82048.) As a member of the Fortuna City Council, you are a public official
within the meaning of the Act.2
Step Two: Are you making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental
decision?
A public official "makes a govemmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.1) A public official
"participates in a governmental decision" when, acting within the auihority of his or her
position and without significant intervening substantive review, the official negotiates,
advises, or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the govenimental
decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use hii or her official
position to influence a decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the official contacts or
appears before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of,his or her agency.
(Regulation 18702.3.) You are making, participating in making, or influen"irg u
governmental decision when acting in your role as a member of the Fortuna City Council.
Step Three: What are your.,economic interests?r,
Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a
govemmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official, a member of his or her immediate family,3 or on any of the offi"iul', economic
interests, described as follows:
a
An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or
indirect investment of $2,000 or more (Section g7103(a); Regulation lg703.l(a));
2 If a public official's office is listed in Section 87200 (*8ZzDfilers" include members of a city
council) and he or she has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she
must: ( l) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, verbally identify each type oieconomic interest
involved in the decision as well as details of the economic intereit, as disiussed in Regulation
18702.5(bX I )(B), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) l-eave the room for rhe
duration ofthe discussion and/or vote on the iiem. For closed sessions, consent calendars, absences and
speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests, special rules found in Regulation lg:oz.5,
subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Section 87105.)
I "Immediate family" is limited to the official's "spouse and dependent children', under
Section 82029.

position of management (Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.1(b)).

From your account of the facts, you appear to have the following economic

decision, you also have an economic interest in the client or customer as a source of
income. (Section 87103(c).)a
Personal Finances
A public official always has an economic interest in his or her personal finances.
A governmental decision will have an effect on this economic interestlf the decision will
result in the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of the official or his or her

Step Four: Are your economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the
decision?
Real Property
Regulation 18704.2(a) states that real property in which a public official has an
economic interest is directly involved in a governmental decision if any of the following
apply:
"(1) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any part
of that real property, is located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or the
proposed boundaries) of the property which is the subject of the
governmental decision . . ..
"(2) The govemmental decision involves the zoning or rezoning , 
'
annexation or deannexation, sale, purchase, or lease, or inclusion in or
exclusion from any city, county, district or other local governmental
subdivision, of the real property in which the official has an interest or a
similar decision affecting the real property. . ..
u However, you have not identified any particular client or customer of Fortuna Marine.
Therefbre, we cannot analyze any potential .onfiittr of interest you may have in a decision affecting a
client or customer. Prior to making, participating in making, or influeniing any governmental decislon you
will need to determine whether any of the clients or customers of Fortuna tvtarine qualify as a source of
income and whether a conflict of interest may arise based upon an economic intereit in one of these clients
or customers. If you have additional questions related to any economic interests in a client or customer of
Fortuna Marine, it is advisable that you request turther assistance providing all relevant facts.
5 A financial efTect on the value of real property owned directly or indirectly by a public official,
and a financial effect onrhe gross revenues, expenses, or value ofassets and liabilities ofa business entity
in which a public ofticial has a direct or indirect investment interest, are not considered separate financial
e-tfects on the official's personal finances and would not be analyzed separately under the:.personal
financial etfects" rule. (Regulation 18705.5(a).) Accordingly, the personal financial efTecti rule does not
appear to apply to your circumstances and we will not discuss it further.
"(3) The governmental decision involves the issuance, denial or
revocation of a license, permit or other land use entitlement authorizing a
specific use or uses of the real property in which the official has an intJrest.
"(4) The governmental decision involves the imposition, repeal, or
modification of any taxes or fees assessed or imposed on the real pioperty in
which the official has an interest.
"(5) The governmental decision is to designate the survey area, [o
select the project area, to adopt the preliminary plan, to form a project area
committee, to certify the environmental document, to adopt the
redevelopment plan, to add territory to the redevelopm"nia.ea, or to rescind
or amend any of the above decisions; and real property in which the official
has an interest, or any part of it is located within the bLundaries (or the
proposed boundaries) of the redevelopment area.
"(6) The decision involves construction of, or improvements to,
streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities, and the real
property in which the official has an interest will receive new or improved
services."
Because your residence, from which you operate Fortuna Marine, is not located
within 500 feet of the property that is the subject of the governmental decision and you
have not provided any facts that the govemmental decision involves any of the typei of
decisions outlined in Regulation 18704.2(aX2)-(6), it appears that yourLconomic interest
in your residence is tndirectly involved in the gou"*rnlrtal decision.6
Fortuna Marine
Regulation 18704.1(a) states that a business entity or source of income is directly
involved in a decision before the official's agency when ihat business entity or source of
income, either directly or by an agent:
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by
filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
"(2) [s a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
conceming the decision before the official or the officiai's ug.n.yl A
person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance,
renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other
entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person.',
u "Ifapublicofficial'seconomicinterestisnotdirectlyinvolvedinagovernmental 
decision,itis
indirectly involved." (Regulation 18704(a).)
Business entities and sources of income that are not directly involved in
governmental decisions are regarded as indirectly involved. (Regulations 18704.1(b),
18705.1(aX2), and Regulation 18705.3(b).) From the facts provided, there is no
indication that Fortuna Marine initiated the proceeding or is a party in, or subject of, the
proceeding within the meaning of Regulation 18704.1. Accordingly, it appears as though
your economic interests in Fortuna Marine, as both a business entity and a source of
income, are indirec,r/y involved in the govemment decision.
Steps Five and Six: Will there be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on your economic interests?
Materiality
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of a
govemmental decision on a public official's economic interests is material. (Regulation
18700(a).) Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on an economic interest will be material, depending on ih" nature of the
economic interest and whether that interest is directly or indirectly involved in the
agency's decision.
Real Property
For properties indirectly involved in a governmental decision, Regulation
18705.2(b)(l) provides that the financial effect is presumed not to be maGrial. However,
this presumption may be rebutted by proof that there are specific circumstances regarding
the governmental decision, its financial effect, and the nature of the real property in
which the public official has an economic interest that make it reasonabty foreseeable the
decision will have a material financial effect on the real property in whic-h the official has
an interest. Examples of these specific circumstances include, but are not limited to,
circumstances where the decision affects:
"(A) The development potential or income producing potential of
the real property in which the official has an economic interest;
"(B) The use of the real property in which the official has an
economic interest;
"(c) The character of the neighborhood including, but not limited
to, substantial effects on: traffic, view, privacy, intensity of use, noise
levels, air emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood.',
Fortuna Marine
For economic interests in business entities indirectly involved in a decision,
including business entities, which are a source of income to an official, the materiality
standard is given at Regulation 18705.1(c).
The thresholds for materiality under this regulation vary with the size of the
business. Based upon the facts you have provided, Regulation 18705.1(c)(4) provides the
applicable materiality threshold for a relatively small business such as Fortuna Marine.
Under this regulation, the financial effect of a govemmental decision on a business is
material if it is reasonably foreseeable that:
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the
amount of $20,000 or more; or,
. 
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating
existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or,
"(C) The govemmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of
$20,000 or more."
Foreseeability
Once a public official has determined the materiality standard applicable to each
of his or her economic interests, the next step is determining whether it ii "reasonably
foreseeable" that the standard will be met. A material finarrcial effect on an economic
interest is "reasonably foreseeable" if it is substantially likely that one or more of the
materiality standards will be met as a result of the govemmental decision. (Regulation
18706(a).) An effect need not be certain to be considered "reasonably foreseeable," but it
must be more than a mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1g75) I Fppc ops. l9g.)
Ultimately, whether a material financial effect is foreseeable at the time a decision
is made depends on facts and circumstances peculiar to each case. (/n re Thorner, supra,
at 198') Because the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice (/n
re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 7l), the foreseeability of a particular financial effect is a
determination that must be left, in most instances, to the informed judgment of the public
official.
Real Propertv
Your property is approximately five miles from Full-on Auto's proposed
building. Barring additional information indicating special circumstan"Lr, ih" financial
effect of the governmental decision on your economic interest in real property is
presumed not to be material under Regulation 18705.2(b)(l). Moreorer, *" irur"
previously advised that the presumption that an indirectly involved property will not be
materially affected is rebutted only if the governmental decision will hive a clearly
distinguishable and substantial financial effect on an official's property. (yang Advice
Letter, No. I-06-198 and Berger Advice Letter, No. A-05-054.) In adopting Regulation
18705.2(b)(l), the Commission noted that "beyond the 500 foot range, participation
would be allowed unless something about the decision makes the official's property
'stick out like a sore thumb."' (Commission Minutes of Meeting, October 6,2iJ00, ut.)
The only fact you have provided that suggests a special circumstance potentially
rebutting the presumption of non-materiality, is the fact that Fortuna Marine is currently
the only outboard marine engine repair shop in the City of Fortuna. However, this
special circumstance is mitigated by the following facts: (1) Full-On Auto provides a full
range of mechanical and fabrication services, (2) Full-On Auto is currentlyoperating in
the city of Eureka just twenty miles from the City of Fortuna, (3) Fortuna tutuii6 onty
repairs engines manufactured by Johnson/Evinrude between 1953 and the 1990,s, ura 1+;
Fortuna Marine is a small business operated from your residence typically handling jusi
one repair job per month. From the facts provided, there is no indicition of special
circumstances to rebut the presumption of non-materiality. Accordingly, it does not
appear reasonably foreseeable that the decision regarding Full-On Auto's proposed
building will have a material financial effect on your economic interest inieai properry.
Fortuna Marine:
As provided in Regulation 18705.1(c)(4), a financial effect of a govemmental
decision of less than $20,000 on the gross revenues of a business entity,-g5,000 on the
expenses of a business entity, or $20,000 on a business entity,s assets or liabilities is not
considered material. Under the facts you have provided, Fortuna Marine,s gross
revenues are just $3,000 to $4,000 a year, while its expenses are $6,000 to $?,000 ayear
Considering your specific circumstances and Fortuna Marine's minimal size, it does not
appear reasonably foreseeable that the govemmental decision regarding Full-On Auto's
proposed building would affect Fortuna Marine's gross revenues, assets, or liabilities at
the $20,000 materiality threshold.
In addition,:r:l though Fortuna Marine's annual expenses exceed $5,000, they
only do so by $1,000-$3,000 per year. Thus, considering your specific circumstances, it
seems equally unlikely that the decision regarding Full-ON Auto's proposed building
would add to or affect these expenses in an amount meeting or e*ceiding the $5,000


File No. 4.-09-210
materiality threshold. Accordingly, we do not think it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect on your economic intirest in Fortuna
Marine.
Steps Seven and Eight: Does this governmental decision come within any exception
to the conflict-of-interest rules?
Even if an official has a conflict of interest, disqualification is not required if the
govemmental decision affects the public official's economic interests in a manner that is
indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision'will affect the public generally.
(Section 87103; Regulation 18707(a).)
Additionally, in certain rare circumstances, a public official may be called upon to
take part in a decision despite the fact that the official may have a disqualifying conflict of
interest under the "legally required participation" exception. This exception uf,pti* only in
certain very specific circumstances where the government agency would Ue paratyzed fiom
acting. (Section 87101; Regulation 18708.)
However, you have not presented any facts indicating that the "public generally,,
or the "legally required participation" exceptions are applicable to youicirc,,rirtun""r, ,o
we will not address them further.

CONCLUSION: You may participate in the decision regarding the proposed building so long as no
additional facts establish a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your
economic interest(s).

CA 95gll_2329 (916) 321-5660 o Fax (916) -322-0886 September 16,2009 Dean Glaser Councilmember City of Fortuna 1546 Ronald Ave. Fortuna, CA 95540 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-09-210 Dear Mr. Glaser: This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").r Nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions. tn addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (,.the Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (ln re Oglesby (r975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest or Govemment Code Section 1090. QrrEsTroN May you make, participate in making, or influence a govemmental decision regarding a proposed building for a business that will offer marine outboard engine repair despite owning a business specializing in marine outboard engine repair approx-imately five miles away from rhe proposed building? ' The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections g1000 through 91014. All statutory ref'erences are to the Government Code. unless otherwise indicated. The regulationi of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections l8 I l0 through I U997 of Title 2 of the Califbrnia Code of Regulations. All regulatory ret'erences are to Title 2. Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. ;i- File No. A-09-2lO Page No. 2 CONCLUSION You may participate in the decision regarding the proposed building so long as no additional facts establish a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your economic interest(s). FACTS You are a member of the Fortuna City Council. You are also the owner of a small business known as Fortuna Marine. This business specializes in the repair of outboard marine engines and is limited to engines manufactured by Johnson/Evinrude between 1953 and the 1990's. As a member of the Fortuna City Council you may be considering a building proposed by Ken Molton, owner of a business known u, iFull-On Auto.i Full-on Auto, which plans to occupy the building at the end of 8th street if approved, specializes in repairing all internal combustion engines including both automotive and marine engines. It also offers various mechanical services including custom fabrication and weldinf. In a telephone conversation on September 2,2009, you further stated that Full-On Auto intends to relocate to the proposed building from its current location in Eureka, California, which is approximately twenty miles from the City of Fortuna. your business, Fortuna Marine, is located approximately five miles from the proposed building and is currently the only outboard marine engine repair shop in the Cityof Fortuna.
